content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:c9f37c46

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains durable facts about the user's stand-up comedy interest, prior open mic experience, joke-writing workflow, and ongoing difficulty area, plus reusable advice/context worth noting.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        - profile benchmark:longmemeval:c9f37c46:profile:<0> Profile for User (USER).
Stable facts:
- The user plans to write new material for the first few open mics and then refine their best jokes as they become more comfortable.
- The user plans to record and review their stand-up performances to identify what they can improve.
- The user wants to try performing stand-up comedy.
- The user plans to attend a few open mics first to get a feel for the scene before deciding whether to take a comedy class.
- The user has been watching stand-up comedy regularly since around February 2023.
- The user wants to see live comedy events.
- The user likes John Mulaney's stand-up comedy.
- note hot-path-1916fefba3c5-note-3: PLAN; Early open mic material strategy; The user's early stand-up material strategy is to write new material for the first few open mics to experiment, then shift toward refining the jokes that perform best as they become more comfortable.; scope=User's approach to developing and refining stand-up material during early open mic performances.
- note hot-path-1916fefba3c5-note-2: PLAN; Begin stand-up via open mics before class; For the user's early stand-up exploration, a practical path is to attend 2–3 open mics first to understand the local scene and test whether performing feels worthwhile, then take a beginner's comedy class for structure and feedback while continuing open mics for stage time.; scope=User's plan for getting started with stand-up comedy and deciding whether to take a comedy class.
- claim hot-path-1916fefba3c5-claim-10: has_goal; The user plans to write new material for the first few open mics and then refine their best jokes as they become more comfortable.; scope=User's stand-up practice plan as of 2023-05-27
- claim hot-path-1916fefba3c5-claim-8: has_goal; The user wants to try performing stand-up comedy.; scope=User comedy-related goals
- claim hot-path-1916fefba3c5-claim-5: has_experience_with; The user has been watching stand-up comedy regularly since around February 2023.; scope=User entertainment history and recommendation context
- claim hot-path-1916fefba3c5-claim-6: prefers; The user likes John Mulaney's stand-up comedy.; scope=User comedy entertainment preferences
- claim hot-path-1916fefba3c5-claim-11: has_goal; The user plans to record and review their stand-up performances to identify what they can improve.; scope=User's stand-up improvement plan as of 2023-05-27
- claim hot-path-1916fefba3c5-claim-9: has_goal; The user plans to attend a few open mics first to get a feel for the scene before deciding whether to take a comedy class.; scope=User's stand-up learning plan as of 2023-05-27
- claim hot-path-1916fefba3c5-claim-7: has_goal; The user wants to see live comedy events.; scope=User comedy-related goals
- note hot-path-1916fefba3c5-note-4: PLAN; Use recordings and selective feedback to improve stand-up; The user plans to record stand-up performances, review them for improvement without being overly self-critical, and may share recordings with trusted comedians or mentors for targeted feedback while filtering subjective or conflicting advice.; scope=User's self-review and feedback workflow for improving stand-up performances.

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<0>; type=existing; name=I; reason=First-person statements in the target message refer to the stable namespace-level User entity.
- mention=stand-up comedy; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<1>; type=existing; name=stand-up comedy; reason=Existing concept exactly matches the mentioned domain.
- mention=open mic night; action=ADD_ALIAS; id=entity:<2>; type=existing; name=open mic night; reason=The mention is a surface form of the existing Open mic concept.
- mention=local comedy club; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=LOCATION; name=Local comedy club; reason=The text refers to a concrete venue, but no existing location candidate safely matches it.
- mention=friends and family; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=CONCEPT; name=Friends and family; reason=The target message discusses testing jokes on this reusable social group, and no existing candidate matches.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|stand-up comedy|open mic night|joke writing
        text_queries=stand-up comedy open mic joke writing notebook friends family relatable mundane situations|writing jokes everyday experiences finding humor in mundane situations|user carries small notebook jotting joke ideas tests jokes on friends and family
        predicate_hints=has_goal|has_experience_with

Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:c9f37c46:session:2; title=LongMemEval c9f37c46 session 2; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/27 (Sat) 13:51
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some advice on writing jokes. I've recently gotten into stand-up comedy and I'm trying to improve my craft. Last month, I finally worked up the courage to attend an open mic night at a local comedy club, and it was amazing to see people trying out their material in front of a live audience.
assistant: Congratulations on taking the first step into the world of stand-up comedy! Attending an open mic night is a great way to get a feel for the stage and see how different comedians approach their craft. Now that you've dipped your toes in, you're probably eager to develop your own material and improve your joke-writing skills. Here are some tips to help you get started:

1. **Write regularly**: Make writing jokes a habit. Set aside time each day or week to brainstorm and write down ideas, even if they're not fully formed. The more you write, the better you'll become at recognizing what works and what doesn't.
2. **Observe life**: Comedy often comes from observing the mundane and finding the humor in everyday situations. Pay attention to your daily experiences, conversations, and surroundings. Take note of things that make you laugh or raise an eyebrow.
3. **Develop your unique voice**: Don't try to emulate another comedian's style; instead, find your own voice and perspective. What makes you unique? What's your take on the world? Your authenticity will shine through in your writing.
4. **Start with a strong premise**: A good joke usually begins with a solid premise or setup. This is the foundation of your joke. Make sure it's clear, concise, and relatable.
5. **Keep it concise**: Don't overexplain or overcomplicate your joke. The punchline should be quick and surprising. Aim for a 3-5 second delay between the setup and punchline.
6. **Punchline, punchline, punchline**: The punchline is the payoff. It should be the funniest part of the joke. Experiment with different punchlines for the same setup to find the one that works best.
7. **Edit and refine**: Once you have a joke, revisit it and refine it. Cut out unnecessary words, tighten up the language, and make sure it flows well.
8. **Test your material**: Try out your jokes on friends, family, or a small group before taking them to the stage. This will help you gauge what works and what needs improvement.
9. **Be authentic and honest**: Write about your own experiences and emotions. Audiences respond to authenticity and vulnerability.
10. **Don't be afraid to bomb**: Not every joke will kill. It's okay to bomb on stage. Use it as an opportunity to learn and improve. Analyze what didn't work and try to fix it.
11. **Study the greats**: Watch and study the work of experienced comedians. Analyze their structure, pacing, and delivery. Learn from their techniques and incorporate them into your own writing.
12. **Keep writing and performing**: The more you write and perform, the better you'll become. Make comedy a habit, and you'll see improvement over time.

Remember, writing jokes is a process, and it takes time to develop your skills. Don't get discouraged if your first draft isn't perfect. Keep writing, refining, and performing, and you'll eventually find your comedic voice.

Good luck, and have fun!
user: Can you give me some tips on how to structure a joke? I've been trying to write jokes, but I'm not sure how to make them flow properly.
assistant: Structuring a joke is crucial to its success. Here are some tips to help you craft a well-structured joke:

1. **The Setup-Punchline Formula**: Most jokes follow a simple setup-punchline structure. The setup provides context and sets up the expectation, while the punchline subverts or plays with that expectation.
	* Setup: Provides context, introduces the premise, and sets up the expectation.
	* Punchline: The unexpected twist, surprise, or payoff.
2. **The Rule of Three**: Many jokes use the rule of three, where you:
	* Introduce the premise or situation
	* Add a twist or escalation
	* Deliver the punchline
3. **Create a Clear Premise**: Your setup should clearly establish the situation, character, or idea. Make sure it's concise and easy to follow.
4. **Keep it Concise**: Aim for a setup that's 1-2 sentences long. Any longer, and you risk losing the audience's attention.
5. **Use a Strong Connector**: The connector is the word or phrase that links the setup to the punchline. Use a strong connector like "but," "however," "meanwhile," or "so" to create a clear transition.
6. **Punchline Placement**: Place the punchline at the end of the sentence or phrase. This creates a natural pause, allowing the audience to process the joke.
7. **Use Surprise and Incongruity**: A good punchline often subverts the audience's expectation or creates an unexpected connection. Use surprise and incongruity to create humor.
8. **Clarity Over Cleverness**: Prioritize clarity over cleverness. Make sure the audience understands the joke before trying to be too clever or wordplay-heavy.
9. **Use Active Voice**: Write in the active voice, as it tends to be more engaging and easier to follow.
10. **Read it Aloud**: Once you've written a joke, read it aloud to yourself. Does it sound natural? Do the words flow well? Make adjustments as needed.

Here's an example of a joke that follows these guidelines:

**Setup:** I told my wife she was drawing her eyebrows too high.
**Connector:** So,
**Punchline:** she looked surprised.

In this joke:

* The setup is concise and clear.
* The connector "so" links the setup to the punchline.
* The punchline is unexpected and plays on the word "surprised."
* The joke follows the rule of three, with the setup, twist, and punchline.

Remember, structuring a joke is an art, and there's no one-size-fits-all approach. Experiment with different structures and styles to find what works best for you.
user: I've been trying to write jokes, but I'm not really sure how to make them relatable. I've been carrying a small notebook with me everywhere and jotting down ideas as they come to me. Some of them are terrible, but a few have potential. I've been testing them out on my friends and family, and it's been great to see what works and what doesn't.
assistant: You're on the right track! Carrying a notebook to jot down ideas is a great habit to get into. And testing your jokes on friends and family is an excellent way to get feedback and refine your material.

Making jokes relatable is crucial to getting laughs from your audience. Here are some tips to help you craft relatable jokes:

1. **Draw from personal experiences**: Share your own stories, struggles, and observations. People can relate to authenticity and vulnerability.
2. **Tap into universal emotions**: Explore common feelings like frustration, excitement, or embarrassment. These emotions are universal and can be applied to various situations.
3. **Find the common ground**: Identify shared experiences or situations that many people can relate to, such as:
	* Relationships (romantic, friendships, family)
	* Work or school struggles
	* Everyday annoyances (traffic, technology, etc.)
	* Common fears or anxieties
4. **Make it specific**: Instead of generalizing, add specific details to make your joke more relatable and tangible. For example:
	* Instead of "I hate mornings," say "I hate when my alarm clock doesn't work, and I'm late for work."
5. **Use shared cultural references**: Tap into popular culture, trends, or memes that your audience is familiar with. This can help create a sense of shared experience and make your joke more relatable.
6. **Exaggerate and embellish**: Take a relatable situation and amplify it for comedic effect. This can make the joke more engaging and memorable.
7. **Be honest and authentic**: Share your own quirks, flaws, and insecurities. This can help create a sense of connection with your audience.

When testing your jokes on friends and family, pay attention to their reactions and feedback. Ask them:

* What did they find relatable about the joke?
* What didn't they understand or connect with?
* How did they feel during the joke (laughed, smiled, confused, etc.)?

Use this feedback to refine your jokes and make them more relatable to a wider audience. Remember, relatability is key to getting laughs and building a connection with your audience.

Keep writing, testing, and refining your jokes. You're on the right track, and with time and practice, you'll develop a unique voice and style that resonates with your audience.
user: That's really helpful. I've been trying to write jokes about my everyday experiences, but I'm not sure how to make them funny. Can you give me some tips on how to find the humor in mundane situations?
assistant: Finding humor in everyday experiences is a crucial skill for any comedian! It's all about perspective and learning to observe the absurdity and irony in the mundane. Here are some tips to help you find the humor in everyday situations:

1. **Look for the absurdity**: Identify the ridiculous or illogical aspects of a situation. Ask yourself, "What's the most absurd thing about this?"
2. **Exaggerate and embellish**: Take a normal situation and amplify it for comedic effect. For example, if you're writing about a long commute, exaggerate the traffic, the delays, or the weird people you encounter.
3. **Find the irony**: Look for contradictions or unexpected twists in everyday situations. Irony can be a great source of humor. For example, "I spent an hour getting ready for the gym, only to realize I forgot my gym clothes."
4. **Observe the details**: Pay attention to the small, often-overlooked details in everyday situations. These can be great sources of humor. For example, "Have you ever noticed how grocery stores always put the milk in the back of the store, forcing you to walk past all the junk food?"
5. **Play with expectations**: Set up a familiar situation, then subvert the audience's expectations. For example, "I went to the coffee shop and ordered a coffee... and they got it right!"
6. **Make connections**: Find unexpected connections between two seemingly unrelated things. This can create humor through surprise and cleverness. For example, "I realized that my relationship is like a pizza delivery – it starts hot, but eventually gets cold and stale."
7. **Find the humor in your reactions**: Instead of just describing a situation, focus on your reaction to it. This can add a layer of humor and relatability. For example, "I was so angry when my internet went out, I almost cried... and then I realized I was just being dramatic."
8. **Use wordplay and language**: Play with language to create humor. Use puns, double meanings, or clever turns of phrase to add humor to your jokes.
9. **Make it personal**: Share your personal quirks and flaws. This can make your humor more relatable and endearing to the audience. For example, "I'm not lazy, I'm just on energy-saving mode... like my phone."
10. **Practice observation**: Carry a journal or take notes on your phone to record your observations throughout the day. Practice observing the world around you and finding the humor in everyday situations.

Remember, humor is subjective, and what's funny to one person might not be funny to another. The key is to find your unique voice and perspective, and to keep writing and refining your jokes until you find what works for you.

Keep in mind that finding humor in everyday situations takes time and practice. Don't be discouraged if it doesn't come easily at first. With time and effort, you'll develop your skills and learn to find the humor in even the most mundane situations.
user: I've been trying to write jokes about my everyday experiences, but I'm struggling to make them funny. Last month, I finally worked up the courage to attend an open mic night at a local comedy club, and it was amazing to see people trying out their material in front of a live audience. I've been trying to apply what I learned from that experience to my own writing, but I'm still having trouble finding the humor in mundane situations. Do you have any tips on how to make my jokes more relatable and funny?
assistant: I'm glad you're taking the leap and trying to make people laugh! Writing jokes about everyday experiences can be a great way to connect with audiences, but it can be tough to make them funny. Here are some tips to help you make your jokes more relatable and hilarious:

1. **Find the unexpected twist**: Take a familiar situation and add an unexpected twist. For example, "I went to the grocery store and saw a sign that said '10 items or less.' I thought, 'Finally, a store that's okay with me being lazy!'"
2. **Use self-deprecation**: Make fun of yourself and your own flaws. Self-deprecation can be relatable and endearing to audiences. For example, "I'm not arguing, I'm just explaining why I'm right. There's a difference... or so I tell myself."
3. **Exaggerate and embellish**: Take a normal situation and amplify it for comedic effect. For example, "I was so tired this morning, I think I slept for 12 hours. Or maybe it was just 5 minutes, but it felt like 12 hours."
4. **Make it specific**: Instead of generalizing, add specific details to make your joke more relatable and tangible. For example, "I hate when I'm driving and the person in front of me is going 25 in a 40 zone. I'm not in a hurry, but I'm also not 95 years old."
5. **Use wordplay and language**: Play with language to create humor. Use puns, double meanings, or clever turns of phrase to add humor to your jokes. For example, "I told my wife she was drawing her eyebrows too high. She looked surprised."
6. **Find the humor in your reactions**: Instead of just describing a situation, focus on your reaction to it. This can add a layer of humor and relatability. For example, "I was so angry when my internet went out, I almost cried... and then I realized I was just being dramatic."
7. **Make it universal**: Find ways to make your joke relatable to a wider audience. For example, "I'm not lazy, I'm just on energy-saving mode... like my phone." This joke is relatable to anyone who's ever felt lazy or struggled with technology.
8. **Practice and refine**: Keep writing and refining your jokes until you find what works for you. Try out different versions of the same joke to see what gets the biggest laugh.
9. **Study other comedians**: Watch and study other comedians' performances. Analyze their structure, pacing, and delivery. Learn from their techniques and incorporate them into your own writing.
10. **Be authentic and honest**: Share your own experiences and emotions. Be vulnerable and honest, and your humor will be more relatable and authentic.

Remember, humor is subjective, and what's funny to one person might not be funny to another. The key is to find your unique voice and perspective, and to keep writing and refining your jokes until you find what works for you.

Keep in mind that it takes time and practice to develop your comedic voice and style. Don't be discouraged if it doesn't come easily at first. Keep writing, trying out new material, and refining your craft. With time and effort, you'll find your groove and make people laugh!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some advice on writing jokes. I've recently gotten into stand-up comedy and I'm trying to improve my craft. Last month, I finally worked up the courage to attend an open mic night at a local comedy club, and it was amazing to see people trying out their material in front of a live audience.
assistant: Congratulations on taking the first step into the world of stand-up comedy! Attending an open mic night is a great way to get a feel for the stage and see how different comedians approach their craft. Now that you've dipped your toes in, you're probably eager to develop your own material and improve your joke-writing skills. Here are some tips to help you get started:

1. **Write regularly**: Make writing jokes a habit. Set aside time each day or week to brainstorm and write down ideas, even if they're not fully formed. The more you write, the better you'll become at recognizing what works and what doesn't.
2. **Observe life**: Comedy often comes from observing the mundane and finding the humor in everyday situations. Pay attention to your daily experiences, conversations, and surroundings. Take note of things that make you laugh or raise an eyebrow.
3. **Develop your unique voice**: Don't try to emulate another comedian's style; instead, find your own voice and perspective. What makes you unique? What's your take on the world? Your authenticity will shine through in your writing.
4. **Start with a strong premise**: A good joke usually begins with a solid premise or setup. This is the foundation of your joke. Make sure it's clear, concise, and relatable.
5. **Keep it concise**: Don't overexplain or overcomplicate your joke. The punchline should be quick and surprising. Aim for a 3-5 second delay between the setup and punchline.
6. **Punchline, punchline, punchline**: The punchline is the payoff. It should be the funniest part of the joke. Experiment with different punchlines for the same setup to find the one that works best.
7. **Edit and refine**: Once you have a joke, revisit it and refine it. Cut out unnecessary words, tighten up the language, and make sure it flows well.
8. **Test your material**: Try out your jokes on friends, family, or a small group before taking them to the stage. This will help you gauge what works and what needs improvement.
9. **Be authentic and honest**: Write about your own experiences and emotions. Audiences respond to authenticity and vulnerability.
10. **Don't be afraid to bomb**: Not every joke will kill. It's okay to bomb on stage. Use it as an opportunity to learn and improve. Analyze what didn't work and try to fix it.
11. **Study the greats**: Watch and study the work of experienced comedians. Analyze their structure, pacing, and delivery. Learn from their techniques and incorporate them into your own writing.
12. **Keep writing and performing**: The more you write and perform, the better you'll become. Make comedy a habit, and you'll see improvement over time.

Remember, writing jokes is a process, and it takes time to develop your skills. Don't get discouraged if your first draft isn't perfect. Keep writing, refining, and performing, and you'll eventually find your comedic voice.

Good luck, and have fun!
user: Can you give me some tips on how to structure a joke? I've been trying to write jokes, but I'm not sure how to make them flow properly.
assistant: Structuring a joke is crucial to its success. Here are some tips to help you craft a well-structured joke:

1. **The Setup-Punchline Formula**: Most jokes follow a simple setup-punchline structure. The setup provides context and sets up the expectation, while the punchline subverts or plays with that expectation.
	* Setup: Provides context, introduces the premise, and sets up the expectation.
	* Punchline: The unexpected twist, surprise, or payoff.
2. **The Rule of Three**: Many jokes use the rule of three, where you:
	* Introduce the premise or situation
	* Add a twist or escalation
	* Deliver the punchline
3. **Create a Clear Premise**: Your setup should clearly establish the situation, character, or idea. Make sure it's concise and easy to follow.
4. **Keep it Concise**: Aim for a setup that's 1-2 sentences long. Any longer, and you risk losing the audience's attention.
5. **Use a Strong Connector**: The connector is the word or phrase that links the setup to the punchline. Use a strong connector like "but," "however," "meanwhile," or "so" to create a clear transition.
6. **Punchline Placement**: Place the punchline at the end of the sentence or phrase. This creates a natural pause, allowing the audience to process the joke.
7. **Use Surprise and Incongruity**: A good punchline often subverts the audience's expectation or creates an unexpected connection. Use surprise and incongruity to create humor.
8. **Clarity Over Cleverness**: Prioritize clarity over cleverness. Make sure the audience understands the joke before trying to be too clever or wordplay-heavy.
9. **Use Active Voice**: Write in the active voice, as it tends to be more engaging and easier to follow.
10. **Read it Aloud**: Once you've written a joke, read it aloud to yourself. Does it sound natural? Do the words flow well? Make adjustments as needed.

Here's an example of a joke that follows these guidelines:

**Setup:** I told my wife she was drawing her eyebrows too high.
**Connector:** So,
**Punchline:** she looked surprised.

In this joke:

* The setup is concise and clear.
* The connector "so" links the setup to the punchline.
* The punchline is unexpected and plays on the word "surprised."
* The joke follows the rule of three, with the setup, twist, and punchline.

Remember, structuring a joke is an art, and there's no one-size-fits-all approach. Experiment with different structures and styles to find what works best for you.
user: I've been trying to write jokes, but I'm not really sure how to make them relatable. I've been carrying a small notebook with me everywhere and jotting down ideas as they come to me. Some of them are terrible, but a few have potential. I've been testing them out on my friends and family, and it's been great to see what works and what doesn't.
assistant: You're on the right track! Carrying a notebook to jot down ideas is a great habit to get into. And testing your jokes on friends and family is an excellent way to get feedback and refine your material.

Making jokes relatable is crucial to getting laughs from your audience. Here are some tips to help you craft relatable jokes:

1. **Draw from personal experiences**: Share your own stories, struggles, and observations. People can relate to authenticity and vulnerability.
2. **Tap into universal emotions**: Explore common feelings like frustration, excitement, or embarrassment. These emotions are universal and can be applied to various situations.
3. **Find the common ground**: Identify shared experiences or situations that many people can relate to, such as:
	* Relationships (romantic, friendships, family)
	* Work or school struggles
	* Everyday annoyances (traffic, technology, etc.)
	* Common fears or anxieties
4. **Make it specific**: Instead of generalizing, add specific details to make your joke more relatable and tangible. For example:
	* Instead of "I hate mornings," say "I hate when my alarm clock doesn't work, and I'm late for work."
5. **Use shared cultural references**: Tap into popular culture, trends, or memes that your audience is familiar with. This can help create a sense of shared experience and make your joke more relatable.
6. **Exaggerate and embellish**: Take a relatable situation and amplify it for comedic effect. This can make the joke more engaging and memorable.
7. **Be honest and authentic**: Share your own quirks, flaws, and insecurities. This can help create a sense of connection with your audience.

When testing your jokes on friends and family, pay attention to their reactions and feedback. Ask them:

* What did they find relatable about the joke?
* What didn't they understand or connect with?
* How did they feel during the joke (laughed, smiled, confused, etc.)?

Use this feedback to refine your jokes and make them more relatable to a wider audience. Remember, relatability is key to getting laughs and building a connection with your audience.

Keep writing, testing, and refining your jokes. You're on the right track, and with time and practice, you'll develop a unique voice and style that resonates with your audience.
user: That's really helpful. I've been trying to write jokes about my everyday experiences, but I'm not sure how to make them funny. Can you give me some tips on how to find the humor in mundane situations?
assistant: Finding humor in everyday experiences is a crucial skill for any comedian! It's all about perspective and learning to observe the absurdity and irony in the mundane. Here are some tips to help you find the humor in everyday situations:

1. **Look for the absurdity**: Identify the ridiculous or illogical aspects of a situation. Ask yourself, "What's the most absurd thing about this?"
2. **Exaggerate and embellish**: Take a normal situation and amplify it for comedic effect. For example, if you're writing about a long commute, exaggerate the traffic, the delays, or the weird people you encounter.
3. **Find the irony**: Look for contradictions or unexpected twists in everyday situations. Irony can be a great source of humor. For example, "I spent an hour getting ready for the gym, only to realize I forgot my gym clothes."
4. **Observe the details**: Pay attention to the small, often-overlooked details in everyday situations. These can be great sources of humor. For example, "Have you ever noticed how grocery stores always put the milk in the back of the store, forcing you to walk past all the junk food?"
5. **Play with expectations**: Set up a familiar situation, then subvert the audience's expectations. For example, "I went to the coffee shop and ordered a coffee... and they got it right!"
6. **Make connections**: Find unexpected connections between two seemingly unrelated things. This can create humor through surprise and cleverness. For example, "I realized that my relationship is like a pizza delivery – it starts hot, but eventually gets cold and stale."
7. **Find the humor in your reactions**: Instead of just describing a situation, focus on your reaction to it. This can add a layer of humor and relatability. For example, "I was so angry when my internet went out, I almost cried... and then I realized I was just being dramatic."
8. **Use wordplay and language**: Play with language to create humor. Use puns, double meanings, or clever turns of phrase to add humor to your jokes.
9. **Make it personal**: Share your personal quirks and flaws. This can make your humor more relatable and endearing to the audience. For example, "I'm not lazy, I'm just on energy-saving mode... like my phone."
10. **Practice observation**: Carry a journal or take notes on your phone to record your observations throughout the day. Practice observing the world around you and finding the humor in everyday situations.

Remember, humor is subjective, and what's funny to one person might not be funny to another. The key is to find your unique voice and perspective, and to keep writing and refining your jokes until you find what works for you.

Keep in mind that finding humor in everyday situations takes time and practice. Don't be discouraged if it doesn't come easily at first. With time and effort, you'll develop your skills and learn to find the humor in even the most mundane situations.
user: I've been trying to write jokes about my everyday experiences, but I'm struggling to make them funny. Last month, I finally worked up the courage to attend an open mic night at a local comedy club, and it was amazing to see people trying out their material in front of a live audience. I've been trying to apply what I learned from that experience to my own writing, but I'm still having trouble finding the humor in mundane situations. Do you have any tips on how to make my jokes more relatable and funny?
assistant: I'm glad you're taking the leap and trying to make people laugh! Writing jokes about everyday experiences can be a great way to connect with audiences, but it can be tough to make them funny. Here are some tips to help you make your jokes more relatable and hilarious:

1. **Find the unexpected twist**: Take a familiar situation and add an unexpected twist. For example, "I went to the grocery store and saw a sign that said '10 items or less.' I thought, 'Finally, a store that's okay with me being lazy!'"
2. **Use self-deprecation**: Make fun of yourself and your own flaws. Self-deprecation can be relatable and endearing to audiences. For example, "I'm not arguing, I'm just explaining why I'm right. There's a difference... or so I tell myself."
3. **Exaggerate and embellish**: Take a normal situation and amplify it for comedic effect. For example, "I was so tired this morning, I think I slept for 12 hours. Or maybe it was just 5 minutes, but it felt like 12 hours."
4. **Make it specific**: Instead of generalizing, add specific details to make your joke more relatable and tangible. For example, "I hate when I'm driving and the person in front of me is going 25 in a 40 zone. I'm not in a hurry, but I'm also not 95 years old."
5. **Use wordplay and language**: Play with language to create humor. Use puns, double meanings, or clever turns of phrase to add humor to your jokes. For example, "I told my wife she was drawing her eyebrows too high. She looked surprised."
6. **Find the humor in your reactions**: Instead of just describing a situation, focus on your reaction to it. This can add a layer of humor and relatability. For example, "I was so angry when my internet went out, I almost cried... and then I realized I was just being dramatic."
7. **Make it universal**: Find ways to make your joke relatable to a wider audience. For example, "I'm not lazy, I'm just on energy-saving mode... like my phone." This joke is relatable to anyone who's ever felt lazy or struggled with technology.
8. **Practice and refine**: Keep writing and refining your jokes until you find what works for you. Try out different versions of the same joke to see what gets the biggest laugh.
9. **Study other comedians**: Watch and study other comedians' performances. Analyze their structure, pacing, and delivery. Learn from their techniques and incorporate them into your own writing.
10. **Be authentic and honest**: Share your own experiences and emotions. Be vulnerable and honest, and your humor will be more relatable and authentic.

Remember, humor is subjective, and what's funny to one person might not be funny to another. The key is to find your unique voice and perspective, and to keep writing and refining your jokes until you find what works for you.

Keep in mind that it takes time and practice to develop your comedic voice and style. Don't be discouraged if it doesn't come easily at first. Keep writing, trying out new material, and refining your craft. With time and effort, you'll find your groove and make people laugh!
